textPlain: Add this to:Falsehoods lawmakers believe about faces.

We're entering a world where hacking facial recognition is going to be a big thing. People are going to start wearing masks in public because of ICE, and to avoid repercussions for going to protests, and that's going to normalize a slippery slope. We're going to end up all Anonymous.

News articles about the OSA always come up with the weirdest and most extreme edge cases when reporting how problematic the law is. "The AI company some website uses has a facial recognition filter that doesn't work on a man that looks more like an injured smurf than a human" isn't all that compelling a problem to have with the "share your screen with random companies before allowing to use a website" law.Face scanning is only one of the options OSA forces companies to choose from. If that's all they offer, it's on the companies to implement a fallback for edge cases like these.
There's a deep irony in that his tattoos make him more uniquely identifiable than the average person.
The chap basically has a permanent version of those clothing items that confuse security cameras.Perhaps we'll see more people sporting this look in the future.
Remove his face mask, not remove his face.I swear, the quality of some journalism is so shameless.
Maybe he could use a Kier Starmer mask.

The article concerns an attention seeker, best denied the oxygen of publicity.If they were to write an article more convincingly, they might compare his difficulties to that of (say) a goth, where the make up is quite heavy and radically transforms appearance.I have not heard of this being a problem with things such as face ID for unlocking phones, which is a very different application due to the added layer of age verification, but still a useful comparison.
But the law doesn't force them to have a fallback.Anyone that the AI doesn't like is therefore excluded from those services.
